title: I, you, and names
pedagogy: Vocabulary: ผม (high ผ + live sonorant final ม → rising; inherent o vowel), ฉัน (high ฉ + live sonorant final น → rising; ◌ั short a), ชื่อ (low ช + mai ek → falling). Concept: gendered first-person pronouns, pronoun dropping, ชื่อ as a verb (no copula). Name chips (แอนนา, มาร์ค) are literal tokens — foreign names, no tone derivation. Reviews คุณ and the polite particles.

exercises.title: “I” depends on who's talking
body: Thai pronouns pick sides: men say ผม, women usually say ฉัน. Same “I”, different speaker — just like ครับ and ค่ะ.
And half the time, nobody says either: Thai drops pronouns whenever context makes them obvious. ชื่อแอนนา alone is a fine “I'm Anna.”
คุณ from Greetings is still your polite “you” — and it doubles as Mr./Ms. before a name.

exercises.explanation: ชื่อ works as the verb — no “is” needed. Subject, ชื่อ, name, particle: that's the whole sentence.
Male speaker, so ผม up front and ครับ at the end — the pronoun and the particle always agree with the speaker.

exercises.context: A woman is introducing herself at a hotel reception.
exercises.question: Which introduction is right?

choices.thai: ฉันชื่อแอนนาค่ะ
ผมชื่อแอนนาค่ะ
ฉันชื่อแอนนาคะ

exercises.explanation: Female speaker → ฉัน and falling-tone ค่ะ. ผม belongs to male speakers, and question-tone คะ doesn't fit a statement.
